revision.comment: Created page with "'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]). == Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabine..."
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Shihan Gakkō]]
== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]
{{Education-stub}} {{Gakkōrei}}

revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:pp]][[category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pp]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

== Related Topics == * [[Shihan Gakkō]]
== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]

== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:pp]][[category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pp]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]

== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:dd]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]

=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:null edit to force updates]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Shihan Gakkō]]
== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Shihan Gakkō]]
== External Links == {{Wikisource|Shihan Gakkōrei}} * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1318073.htm Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)]
{{Education-stub}} {{Gakkōrei}}

== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.

revision.text:
'''Shihan Gakkōrei''' (師範学校令, Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of April 10, Meiji 19) was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] during the [[Meiji]] period of [[Japan]] as an [[imperial ordinance]] under the so-called [[Gakkōrei]] series. It regulated [[teacher training schools]] ([[Shihan Gakkō]]).
== Overview == === Promulgation === Promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 13 of Meiji 19) under [[Prime Minister]] [[Itō Hirobumi]] (First Itō Cabinet) and [[Minister of Education]] [[Mori Arinori]].
The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] was issued alongside the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 14) and [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 15).
=== Key Features === The ordinance classified [[Shihan Gakkō]] into two categories: [[Higher Normal Schools]] (高等師範学校) and [[Ordinary Normal Schools]] (尋常師範学校).
; Higher Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school in Tokyo'''. * Tokyo Normal School was restructured into the [[Tokyo Higher Normal School]] (later renamed; now [[University of Tsukuba]]). Tokyo Women’s Normal School became the Women's Department of the Higher Normal School but was separated in [[1890]] to become the [[Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School]] (predecessor of [[Ochanomizu University]]). * Expenses were covered by the '''[[national treasury]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools'''.
; Ordinary Normal Schools * Required to establish '''one school per prefecture'''. * Existing normal schools’ elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses<ref>The "Outline of Normal School Curriculum" (Ordinance No. 29 of August 19, 1881) defined graduates' qualifications for elementary, intermediate, and advanced courses.</ref> were integrated into Ordinary Normal Schools. * Expenses were funded through '''[[local taxes]]'''. * Graduates were primarily appointed as '''principals and teachers of public elementary schools'''.
Tuition for students was provided by the schools, encouraging economically disadvantaged individuals to enroll in Normal Schools.
=== Partial Revisions === * [[1887]]: "Additions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 30 of Meiji 20) * [[1891]]: "Revisions to the Shihan Gakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 216 of Meiji 24)
=== Abolition === The [[Shihan Kyōiku Rei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 346 of October 9, Meiji 30), enacted on [[April 1, 1898]], replaced the Shihan Gakkōrei.
== Content == ; Article 1 (Purpose) Shihan Gakkō shall train individuals to become teachers, with emphasis on nurturing students' qualities of integrity, loyalty, and dignity. ; Article 2 (Categories) Divided into Higher Normal Schools and Ordinary Normal Schools, the former under the jurisdiction of the [[Minister of Education]]. ; Article 3 (Establishment) One Higher Normal School in Tokyo and one Ordinary Normal School per prefecture were mandated. ; Article 4 (Funding) Expenses for Higher Normal Schools came from the national treasury, while those for Ordinary Normal Schools were funded by local taxes. ; Article 5 (Ordinary Normal School Budget) Local tax allocations for Ordinary Normal Schools required prefectural approval and authorization from the Minister of Education. ; Article 6 (Tenure of Teachers) Principals and teachers of Shihan Gakkō were appointed for five-year terms, extendable beyond this period. ; Article 7 (Concurrent Duties) Ordinary Normal School principals could concurrently serve as educational administrators within their prefectures. ; Article 8 (Student Recruitment) Regulations for student admission and post-graduation service were determined by the Minister of Education. ; Article 9 (Tuition) Tuition for Normal School students was funded by the schools. ; Articles 10 & 11 (Graduate Appointments) Higher Normal School graduates were appointed as principals and teachers of Ordinary Normal Schools, while Ordinary Normal School graduates were appointed to public elementary schools. ; Article 12 (Curriculum) Curriculum and textbooks were determined by the Minister of Education.
== Related Rules and Regulations == === Ordinary Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * May 26: Curriculum for male students. * May 28: Rules for student recruitment and post-graduation service. * June 4: Tuition rules for male students. * [[1888]]: Standards for school facilities. * [[1892]]: Curriculum revised to include female students; rules for enrollment quotas and simplified teacher training courses.
=== Higher Normal Schools === * [[1886]]: * October 14: Rules for male and female departments, with three-year terms for male students and four years for female students. * [[1894]]: * April 6: Curriculum revisions consolidating departments. * October 2: Separate curriculum for the Tokyo Women’s Higher Normal School.
== Literary Mention == In [[Natsume Sōseki]]'s novel ''[[Botchan]]'', a scene depicts a brawl between [[Kyūsei Chūgakkō]] students and Normal School students. The passage humorously refers to the financial constraints mentioned in Article 4.
